topic: group project nightmare — no one is contributing

turns.content: i am going to commit a crime. specifically against my group project members
Uh oh. What have they done — or more accurately, what have they NOT done?

so we have a group report due in two weeks right. five people in the group. we divided sections THREE WEEKS AGO. i have finished my section. one other person has done like half of theirs. the other three have done literally nothing. NOTHING. not a single word in the shared doc

Three weeks and three out of five people haven't written a single word? That's genuinely infuriating. There's "a bit behind" and then there's "haven't even started with two weeks to go."

Have you reached out to them about it, or has there been radio silence all around?

i sent a message in the group chat a week ago like 'hey just checking in on everyone's progress!' all friendly and non-confrontational and TWO of them left me on read and the third said 'yeah ill get onto it this weekend' and then did not get onto it this weekend. i cannot

The classic "I'll get onto it this weekend" followed by absolute silence. Okay, you're past the polite check-in stage. I'd suggest:

1. **One more message, much more direct** — not aggressive but clear. Mention the peer evaluation if your unit has one, because that sometimes lights a fire under people.
2. **Email your coordinator** — not to dob, but frame it as seeking advice. It creates a paper trail that protects you.
3. **Align with the one person who IS trying** — two people with a backup plan is way better than one person panicking alone.

Does the unit have peer evaluation?

theres a peer evaluation thing at the end which is something but it doesnt change the fact that if the report is bad we ALL get a bad mark. like my section could be pristine and three missing sections still tank the whole thing. and this is due basically the same week as my thesis so the timing is genuinely comical. universe said let me give this girl every deadline at once

That timing is brutal. Definitely don't sacrifice your thesis time for other people's laziness — prioritize the thing that's worth more to YOUR future.

Document everything — screenshots of the group chat, shared doc edit history. The peer evaluation is your safety net but you need evidence. Have you talked to Jess about a strategy yet?

jess is the one whos done half her section and shes equally furious. we had a rant session about it yesterday and it was very therapeutic lol. she reckons we should just divide their sections between us and do the whole thing ourselves which is tempting but also... no?? like why should we do five peoples work for the same mark

Jess's instinct makes sense from a "save the grade" perspective but you're absolutely right to push back. Doing their work rewards their laziness and burns you out right before your thesis is due. That's a lose-lose for you.

The better play is: send the direct message, email the coordinator with evidence, and make it clear in writing that you and Jess have completed your sections. If the others don't contribute, the peer evaluation and your paper trail should protect your individual marks. Some coordinators will even adjust grades when there's clear evidence of unequal contribution.

Do you know if your coordinator is the type to actually intervene?

honestly i have no idea because ive never had to escalate before... ive always just been the person who quietly picks up the slack which is probably why this keeps happening to me lol. i literally attract group project freeloaders like a magnet. every single semester without fail

That pattern is so common for people who are visibly competent and reliable — freeloaders can sense who'll pick up the slack, and they gravitate toward them. The fact that you've always quietly handled it before means this might actually be a good moment to break that cycle.

Sending the email isn't dobbing, it's self-advocacy. And honestly, coordinators usually appreciate being told early rather than getting five conflicting stories after marks come out. Frame it as "I want to make sure we submit quality work, can you advise on how to handle unresponsive group members" — professional and solution-oriented.

ok yeah that framing is good. i also lowkey want to just name and shame in the group chat like 'hi dylan hi priya hi tom the doc shows zero edits from any of you since september 24th just so we're all on the same page' but i know thats petty

It IS petty but it's also... factual? There's a version of that message that's direct without being aggressive. Something like: "Hey everyone, just flagging that the shared doc shows contributions from me and Jess only. We need everyone's sections by [specific date] or we won't have time to edit and format before submission. Can everyone confirm they're on track?"

The specific date is key — it creates accountability without being confrontational. And if they still don't respond, that screenshot goes straight into your coordinator email.
THANK YOU i will die on that hill. ok im going to message the group being very direct, talk to jess about a backup plan, and email the coordinator. thanks for talking me off the crime ledge lol bye
No crimes necessary — just strategic directness and documentation. Good luck with the wrangling, and seriously, protect your thesis time. You've got this!
